questions.text: I tend to spend money on experiences and tools that further my personal growth.
I find that I don't need much recovery time after emotionally charged situations.
I tend to frame requests as expectations rather than options.
I set up automated reminders for bills, renewals, and recurring obligations.
I routinely make choices that protect and advance my own interests.
I believe that the professionals who navigate human tragedy most effectively are those who have mastered the discipline of emotional non-engagement.
I feel that personal accountability is the engine that drives real progress.
I tend to use my vacation days to visit people who need company rather than destinations I want to see.
I often find out about interpersonal tensions after they have already escalated.
I believe that diminishing my own visibility allows others to shine.
I hold the view that the moral urgency of suffering is the most compelling argument for action that I ever encounter.
I tend to assert boundaries for the group about what is and isn't acceptable.
I sense when rapport between two people is genuine versus performative.
I detect when a person's written punctuation changes to signal irritation, such as periods becoming curt.
My emotional landscape is always vivid, always moving, always demanding my attention.
I believe that emotional composure during difficult situations is a quality that allows me to serve as a steady point of reference for others.
I routinely update my personal goals and adjust my life to match them.
When I connect deeply with someone, the intensity of that bond registers as a physical sensation.
I feel that the moral claim of suffering remains intact even when the person suffering is unable to articulate their need.
I save when it feels comfortable and spend more freely when opportunities arise.
I rarely detect when someone is strategically timing a remark.
I tend to consult the team before making any scope changes mid-project.
I notice that my emotional responses are like ripples on a pond rather than crashing waves.
I track the time I spend on different activities to understand where my hours go.
I feel that yielding space to others is a natural extension of caring about the group.
I often establish the norms and expectations within a group.
I hold the view that my emotional reactivity is simply the volume setting I was born with.
I sense when a person withholds a compliment that would be natural because of a competitive dynamic.
I rarely take notes, preferring to trust my memory and instincts.
I believe that reading too much into people's behavior leads to misunderstandings.
I feel that my life has more meaning when I am actively engaged in reducing someone else's suffering.
I work in intense bursts of energy rather than at a steady, measured pace.
I find that I often refine and build on ideas others have introduced.
I find that I can maintain equanimity even when those around me are emotional.
I am able to consider the suffering of an individual as one input among many when making decisions that affect a larger group.
I routinely give up my free time so that others can get what they need.
